Not sure what you mean here with visualization?
Usually the library expects you to augment images batch by batch, so that your memory only has to be able to handle one batch of images at a time. That 21k number sounds like you are pre-generating lots of augmented images before starting the training. That is bound to consume lots of RAM and the library can't really do anything about it. Your options would be to change your code to augment every batch on the fly or to save the 21k images and dynamically load them per batch from the filesystem (this will consume lots of space and potentially be slow) or to compress the 21k images to JPEG in memory and then decompress them per batch (this will introduce JPEG compression artifacts and might not save much space for small images).

This presentation from Apple ([slides](http://devstreaming.apple.com/videos/wwdc/2015/719ui2k57m/719/719_your_app_and_next_generation_networks.pdf?dl=1) -- starting page 66, [video / transcript](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2015/719/?time=2214)) has some useful further information. I'll summarize for posterity:

They point out another example of where this is important: applications that can dynamically adjust the data they send to match the network. E.g., streaming video where if you're short on bandwidth you can lower the video quality, or something like VNC where you can drop the refresh rate to preserve interactivity. (Their demo is Apple Screen Sharing having 3 seconds of lag on a connection with a 35 ms ping.) [xpra](http://xpra.org) is a Python remote display app that works like this. Basically the core loop goes like:

```python
while True:
    await sock.writeable()
    # at the last possible moment, take a screenshot
    data = take_screenshot()
    await sock.sendall(data)
```
(Notice: an app like this actually needs `sock.writeable` available as a primitive even if it isn't using TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT.)

What's interesting about this example is it explains *why* the designers decided to make `select` and `send` disagree about whether the socket is writeable: if your proper low water mark is like 8 KiB (plausible), but your protocol means that it's meaningless to send chunks smaller than, say, 100 KiB (VNC sending half-a-screenshot doesn't necessarily make sense), then you don't want to break that 100 KiB chunk into 8 KiB pieces and loop back and forth through the kernel/userspace/scheduler dribbling them out. What you want is to wait until the send buffer is *almost* empty (with `select` or whatever), then enqueue one full chunk, then go to sleep until the send buffer is *almost* empty again.

And finally, they argue that TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT is basically always a win, it's just a question of how much. In fact, I'll go ahead and quote:

> And we started making slides for this presentation where we had two columns. We had the apps that should use the low-water mark option and the apps that shouldn't. And we couldn't think of any to go in the shouldn't column. Every time we thought of a traditional app like file transfer, well, that doesn't need it. We realized you've had that experience where you change your mind about a file transfer and you press Control-C and it seems to take about 30 seconds to cancel. It's because it had over-committed all of this data into the kernel and it had to wait for it to drain because there's no way to change your mind. So, yeah, actually, file transfer does not benefit from over-committing data, and we couldn't think of any application that does benefit from over-stuffing the kernel.
>
> So once we had that realization, we decided starting in the next seed [= what Apple calls a beta release], this option will be turned on automatically for all connections using the higher layer NSURLSession and CFNetwork APIs. All you have to do to make best use of this is when your socket becomes writable, don't loop writing as much data as you can until you get an EWOULDBLOCK. Just write a sensible-sized unit, and then wait to be told it's time for more.

------

That all makes perfect sense to me, so I've changed my mind :-). Methods like `send` and `sendall` should *not* check for writeability, and there should be some public API like `await sock.writeable()`. Also, it probably would be a nice extra bonus to automatically enable TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT whenever possible (i.e. recentish Linux / OS X), with some reasonable default buffer size like 8 or 16 KiB.

I think this would add too much complexity to pint. Right now, the rules for adding SI prefixes are very simple. Adding this feature would require:

* Adding the ability to categorize prefixes and units into groups (SI, not SI, binary, etc).
* Specifying which names are abbreviations (like "ft" for feet), and which are just plural or synonyms (like foot).
* Specifying that a unit has a builtin prefix (like in your kph example).

I think that @hgrecco is correct when he says that "we are all consenting adults," which is also part of the philosophy of Python.

If this could be implemented very simply, and was somehow optional, then I think it would be very neat. I just don't see a way to do it that easily.

DNS over TCP includes a 2 byte length field at the start of the TCP PDU which is not present when using UDP. If you remove it, dpkt will parse your record. RFC is [here](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7766.txt) (see Section 8).


> but Destination ID should always be set to the correct device address.

Not sure on this. EnOceanSerialProtocol3 describes the Destination ID as:

> Broadcast radio: FF FF FF FF
> ADT radio: Destination ID (= address)

All my devices (incl. the Thermokon message server communicating with the HVAC clients) use the broadcast address in their packets (at least this is what our enocean library tells me). 

I guess it does not matter what to use and I also don't know which approach woud be better.

In most rendering a page is slow, it will wait till every resources in the page loaded.
And there are a lot of reason a script will fail, including:

* element not exists when execute
* timeout arrived before page load, don't have time to execute js script
* js script executed, but further resources don't have time or just not loaded
* js script executed, but page js not loaded, doesn't reaction as expected.

> I think I've found all the remaining issues. First, apparently find_package() on windows has the special behavior of searching recent build folders. So if you build dlib somewhere and then try to compile the example programs with a CMakeLists.txt file that uses find_package(dlib) it will find the dlibConfig.cmake file in your recent build folder and try to use it. But that results in an error on the line that says include("${dlib_CMAKE_DIR}/dlib.cmake") since that isn't the right path. I'm sure this error would confuse windows users so it should be addressed. The best thing to do is probably to put the config files inside some sub-folder rather than right in the cmake binary dir, so find_package() won't see them.

```
 def priority(s):
@@ -85,7 +86,9 @@ def priority(s):
     if issubclass(type(s), type):
         return TYPE
     if hasattr(s, 'validate'):
-        return VALIDATOR

                 return data
```

Perhaps you are not properly closing the connections? Most routers/switches only allow a limited number of concurrent sessions. A way to test would be to log into the device manually and (if Cisco IOS) do 'show connections' while your script runs. If you notice multiple sessions using your script's credentials, this is likely the problem.

Take a look at this blog post for a 'clean' way to handle connections -- http://packetpushers.net/using-python-context-managers/

@craffel 
> Hmm. The nearest neighbor idea is good, but I could see it getting ugly and potentially doing the wrong thing, depending on what exactly the metrics are doing (I'm not familiar). Can we just require that reference and estimated annotations have the same timebase like MIREX, thereby also sidestepping this issue?

That's definitely an option, but as a matter of taste I personally don't like the constraint of having to have the same time steps ;). The metrics are all computed per time step and averaged across the song, and nothing. Any problems introduced by resampling would in theory be the same as those introduced when resampling the melody time series.

@bmcfee 
> Is there anything in the task definition that defines what a "contour" is in this representation? 

Nope, that's me adding terminology. afaik, multif0 metrics only care about the bundle of pitches in each time frame, not about any sort of continuity over time.

>Would it make sense to match f0 values at one row to those in the previous row if they are within some maximum cent deviation? Then you could line up all values in one contour to a single column (padded out with nans) and resample as normal.

I see what you're saying. If a "contour" is defined such that the change in pitch from one frame to the next is bounded by some value, then you could potentially stitch things back together. I think that would work most of the time, but break down if contours overlap in pitch, and the decision becomes ambiguous. In any case, multi-f0 eval doesn't care about contours for the moment (perhaps it should in the future)...
If we wanted to actually have "contours" we'd need the algorithms to explicitly output them in a form that groups pitches in a meaningful way, but this isn't how the problem is currently formulated.

Running from the top:
- You're using `client` for the command decorator. While this doesn't necessarily mean you've done something wrong, it implies you are using a `discord.Client` directly, rather than a `commands.Bot`.
  - `discord.Client` on its own does not have the commands extension. Command functionality only exists natively in `commands.Bot` (which is itself a subclass of `discord.Client`).
  - In the event you are using `commands.Bot` somewhere, you may accidentally be using both `Client` and `Bot` when you really only want `Bot` (since it is a subclass of `Client`). If you define an instance for both, remove the `Client` and replace your decorators, etc to use `bot` instead.
- You accept a `message` object, but your decorator says you want a `Context` instead (and in absence of the decorator kwarg, you would not receive an argument at all). You should change this argument and use `ctx.message` to access message attributes.
- You shouldn't be using `process_commands` in a command itself. If your command was actually working, this would cause recursive behavior, sticking your bot into an infinite loop of executing this command.
- You set `message.author` as a field value, but it wants a string. You should do `str(message.author)` or `message.author.mention`, etc to get the desired string for this value.
- You never actually send your embed in this command. You create it, but then nothing else is done. Provided it's supposed to go into the source channel, you can send it by using `await bot.say(embed=em6)`.

* Just don't send 4GB messages as 1 message. If it's something around 10-20MB its ok, but not GB.
* Of course even 10s of MB are more than you can handle by default. See  http://stackoverflow.com/questions/21020347/kafka-sending-a-15mb-message for details. It's old, so your config would differ, but the idea is the same. TLDR:
```
You need to adjust three (or four) properties:

Consumer side:fetch.message.max.bytes - this will determine the largest size of a message that can be fetched by the consumer.
Broker side: replica.fetch.max.bytes - this will allow for the replicas in the brokers to send messages within the cluster and make sure the messages are replicated correctly. If this is too small, then the message will never be replicated, and therefore, the consumer will never see the message because the message will never be committed (fully replicated).
Broker side: message.max.bytes - this is the largest size of the message that can be received by the broker from a producer.
Broker side (per topic): max.message.bytes - this is the largest size of the message the broker will allow to be appended to the topic. This size is validated pre-compression. (Defaults to broker's message.max.bytes.)
```
* Don't use multiprocessing if you're only sending data without compression or preprocessing. Multiprocessing will copy your messages to new processes and spawning/killing processes will make it slow. Just create 1 producer and send data to it, the task should not be CPU bound.

Also what version of the broker are you using? If it's the newest one (0.10.1+) it should handle large messages without fetch_message_max_bytes

The problem with `CrossDown` and any other indicator is that *indicators* **MUST** work with objects of same length. Because *indicators* can take anything which is a *lines* object as input, for example: other indicators.

As such and even if an indicator reference the incoming *lines* object as `self.data`, it may be dealing with *anything* and this *anything* must no longer have a reference to anything else.

The real problem here is that `backtrader` supports 2 methods of execution:

  - `runonce=True` indicators are precalculated in batch mode to accelerate things and then the `Strategy` is executed step-by-step (`next`) by moving indicators' pointers forward as needed. This also precludes the definition of indicators in a recursive manner.

  - `runonce=False`. In this case everything is run step-by-step.

You can always compare values from indicators of different lengths in the `next` method of the strategy.

The "different length" topic is always under consideration, but it may not have a full solution given the nature of the problem itself

The problem is how to reference a scan in a SPEC data file when there is more than one instance of that scan.  While not common, this happens when then `SCAN_N` variable is regressed in SPEC, usually an unintended consequence of some user action.

In PyMCA, the first instance of scan N is referenced as an integer.  Subsequent references are referenced by a float N.1, N.2, ...  We could do that here but users will write code assuming that all references are integers.  In such cases, they will only access scan N.

Now, the method `spec2nexus.spec.SpecDataFile.getScanNumbers()` returns `[str]`.  With the change, this method would return a list of numbers that are either `int` or `float`.  Similar for `spec2nexus.spec.SpecDataFile.getScanNumbersChronological()` except that the items will be in chronological order.

@rayosborn, @JPHammonds:
Should the current implementation of the scan reference be changed back to `int` (or `float` as described above) using this scheme?  Keep in mind the `spec2nexus.spec.SpecDataFile.getScan()` method handles this transparently.

Thanks for scrutinizing the code. As far as I can see, this line defines a starting condition, which is adopted when an instance of that model is created. It can be changed at any point (see code example below) and may be a good or bad assumption for the actual data you have. I think the reason it was implemented like this, is that in the special case of a circular orbit (e=0), it makes the time of periastron passage (tau) the central transit time, which may be convenient.

Cheers

```

# Import some unrelated modules
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pylab as plt
# ... and now the forTrans module
from PyAstronomy.modelSuite import forTrans as ft


# Create MandelAgolLC object with
# circular orbit and quadratic limb darkening
ma = ft.MandelAgolLC(orbit="keplerian", ld="quad")

# See the available parameters and their current values
ma.parameterSummary()

# Set parameters
ma["per"] = 1.0
ma["i"] = 90.0
ma["a"] = 6.5
ma["tau"] = 0.0
ma["p"] = 0.16
ma["linLimb"] = 0.6
ma["quadLimb"] = 0.0
ma["b"] = 0.

t = np.linspace(-0.5,0.5,500)
m1 = ma.evaluate(t)

# Now change w
ma["w"] = +123.7

# See the available parameters and their current values
ma.parameterSummary()

m2 = ma.evaluate(t)

plt.plot(t, m1, 'b.-')
plt.plot(t, m2, 'r.-')
plt.show()


```

Hey, sorry for the long delay before responding, haven't been feeling too well lately!

I think this is a nice addition to the mustache spec but, since it isn't standard, if speed takes a hit then I'll have to refuse this. Speed and implementing the entire spec were the biggest reasons for making chevron as opposed to using what was already available (Plus... I've always wanted to make a compiler and mustache is easy to compile).

That said, I'm very interested in trying this out and seeing if we can get something we like while maintaining spec compliance and speed. One thing I don't like about this (I'm allowed to have complaints because it isn't spec, right?), is that it adds the ```<``` tag which is different from the ```>``` tag. This is just begging for confusion, and I actually didn't notice for the first 30ish minutes of looking at this. 

I'd suggest we go with unifying ```<``` and ```>```, basically if a partial tag (```<``` or ```>```) has a matching end tag then it will be a "parent"/"super" tag, if it doesn't then it will be a normal partial tag like it is now. This would require a change on the tokenizer, right now the tokenizer is a generator of flat tuples containing two elements, the type of the tag and the value inside the tag. so ```{{#test}}hey{{/test}}``` would generate ```('section', 'test'), ('literal', 'hey'), ('end', 'test')```. Instead I would like to suggest turning the tokenizer into a function instead of a generator, and return a list of nested tags instead. The previous example would return this instead
```
[{ 'type': 'section',
           'value': 'test',
           'children': [{ 'type': 'literal', 
                          'value': 'hey'}
                          ]}
]
```

I don't know what kind of performance hit turning the generator into a function would cause, I would imagine it would have little effect though, especially because I don't support streaming the output of render anyways.

We would need this new structure of the tokenizer so that we can efficiently check if a tag is closed or not while tokenizing by looking at the list we are creating, and not need to worry about it in the rendering function.

I'm open to comments but I think this is a backwards compatible and efficient way to do it. I'll start implementing it in the next few days, but if you have any concerns feel free to voice them.

@alecthomas, I understand and respect your decision. At the same time, I think that Voluptuous is a bit inconsistent in that if you intend for it to only support "data", which I take to mean basic data types such as strings and numbers, but :
* Voluptuous also supports some file system functions, which seem outside the traditional definition of "data".
* Voluptuous even goes further by supporting `Object`s as both input and output.
* Finally, Voluptuous has a fairly good understanding of Python's object model as demonstrated by support for `__slots__`.

All these considerations, to me, seem at odds with your current conclusion to not support object references. Beyond that, I think that refactoring the code to allow for a state more robust than `path = list()` (which is what is required to support the proposed requirement) could potentially alleviate what seem to me to be less than desirable features:
* Validator `callable` objects, such as `Schema`, `Msg`, and `Coerce`, have inconsistent signatures with the "compiled" functions generated in `schema_builder.py`
* `error.Invalid.prepend` is a bit odd in that the represented `path` is momentarily incorrect.
* (more-so than the others, this is a bit of a stretch) you could even use a state object `with` `__enter__` and `__exit__` to accumulate errors rather than stuffing them into a `list`.

@ibigpapa Just from the shortness of your text, I strongly suspect your grammar. (And I apologize that debugging grammars is presently so hard. It's an area I intend to make major strides in someday.)

I suggest you focus your thought on recursive areas of your grammar, like this:

```
    block = generic_block
    generic_block = statement block_start line+ block_end
    line = (block / comment / statement)
```

In the above, `block` looks for a `generic_block`, which looks for a `line`, which looks for a `block` again. This may be what you intended, but some cycle like that is almost certainly happening in an *un*intended place, driving you to overdeep recursion.

There, that should help for the moment. When somebody (or I) have a few more minutes, we could make the message more helpful in the case of an unnamed rule by having it suggest you define a generic_visitor or name the subexpression (which we can also call out into the error message by using its repr).

Also, note that `attrs`' `these` parameter is roughly equivalent to the `dataclasses.make_dataclass()` function. So I think the only real difference in your table is `__slots__`, `validate`, and `convert`. I deliberately don't want to support validation and conversion, instead leaving that to static type checkers (see https://github.com/ericvsmith/dataclasses/issues/60#issuecomment-342009693 above).

As for `__slots__`, that's a deliberate decision. Although I have another decorator which I'm not including in the PEP that adds `__slots__` and returns a new class. See `add_slots()` in `dataclass_tools.py` in this repo. Because it's the only parameter that causes `dataclass()` to return a new class, I thought it was best to leave it out, at least for now. I'd like to make sure `dataclass()` is seen as something that just adds methods to a class, not returns a new class. Maybe that will change over time.

Hi Kerry,

Actions are executed during parsing, when a match is recognized. That's why you get one print, it happens when `accept.request` is parsed.
You are trying to evaluate the returned value of accept statement in the while loop. The return value is None (which is returned by accept_statement lambda). Thus, None gets evaluated in the loop.

What you can do is to return a function that do printing from the accept_statement actions and than call that function from the while loop. Something like:

```python
expression, statements = nodes[1], nodes[2]
while (expression):
   for statement in statements:
       statement()
```

I suspect the package info is missing due to the way the release process worked.
Travis is handling the process of pushing a new release to PyPI. A push to PyPI happened unconditionally on every build for every Python version [during a release](https://travis-ci.org/wndhydrnt/python-oauth2/builds/182998989). Usually, Travis scheduled Python 2.6 before all other versions so the release was handled by Python 2.6. I suspect that Python 2.6 is not able to provide the package info to PyPI (anymore).
I tested creating a new release on PyPI Test using Python 3.6 and it worked there.
This issue has led to two changes:

- Support of Python 2.6 has been dropped (https://github.com/wndhydrnt/python-oauth2/commit/36506b9b1dc31ec0b7e1ca4a98d4076adc810d85)
- A new release is now only created when Travis is running Python 3.6 (https://github.com/wndhydrnt/python-oauth2/commit/f99e4ac4cdb74862cbe1b66a09bdac9ea29569cd)

Therefore it's not possible to reset the pandas `max_rows` option after the block has executed.

I think changing the pandas `max_rows` option without changing it back is unacceptable, because in that case setting `displaylimit` on SQL magic would mysteriously change all of pandas' behavior elsewhere as well.

Therefore I suggest allowing the user to manually handle the pandas max_rows option.

this may seem weird, but I'm using the quoting style formerly proposed by Douglas Crockford:
"Single quotes for internal strings and double quotes for strings readable by humans".
This can be a big advantage, whenever one has to search for strings visible in other natural languages.
You therefore might see statements in my code such as:

```
color = ChoiceField(choices=[('red', "Red"), ('green', "Green"), ('blue', "Blue")])
```
This of course also applies to docstrings:
```
def foo():
    """Do foo stuff"""
```

Hi lczub/Luiko,

a) TestGenReporter doesn't have hard coded url or dev key. It's goal is to present the most common use case of always reporting a result if at all possible. In my mind it's value is if other fields are generatable in the future someone's code wouldn't have to be changed in order to get those new benefits. They would simply update this dependency. This model is sometimes used in security repos where there are default objects that will update with the spec. I believe passlib does this.

b) I've moved and provided better docs for TestGenReporter. Not importing the functional classes from TestReport is intentional. They started as and are still mostly intended as Mixin classes. I never intended for them to be used in isolation, but after writing them realized some organizations might want only that one functionality. It would be a little silly to force them to create a new mixin instead of importing the functional one. i.e. why force `MyTestReporter = TestReporter(AddTestCaseReporter)` when they can simply import the class that adds that functionality `AddTestCaseReporter`.

However, I still think that is an edge case. The vast majority of users will be using the classes effectively as mixins which are only adding functionality, which is why the classes have function/method style names. I understand not everyone likes mixin design or naming functions so am open to change, but that was my reasoning.

c) That's a better name, done. The naming style is explained above in b.

c2) I don't believe it does. Is this talking about the docstring? In my experience most teams using this will have an automation dev key and a single testlink server they are setting it up with. If that's not your experience I would be open to changing the docstring.

d+e) I can try to do this when I have more time.

f) Changed to TLArgError.

Ok, I figured it out.  In my `FirehoseDateHourTask`, the `firehose_files_for_date_hour()` function returns the current files that match some criteria in S3.  However, new files can be added to that while the Luigi pipeline is running.  Because these files change, the first time it runs `FirehoseDateHourTask.requires` (when determining which tasks to run) and the second time it runs (when determining if all the tasks have finished running) the list of tasks may have grown and there really are unfulfilled new tasks.

I solve this by caching the list of files the first time `requires` is run and referring only to the cache.

experiment one:

change pattern from:
```python
class ...:
    def __bytes__(self):
        _bytes = bytearray() + super(...).__bytes__()
        _bytes += ...
        ...
        return bytes(_bytes)
```

to:
```python
class ...:
    def __bytearray__(self):
        _bytes = super(...).__bytearray__()
        _bytes += ...
        ...
        return _bytes

    def __bytes__(self):
        return bytes(self.__bytearray__())
```

applied to the following classes:
 - Packet
 - packet.types.Header
 - UserAttribute
 - UserAttributeSubPackets
 - SubPacket
 - packet.subpackets.types.Header
 - Image

I encountered a similar issue when attempting to run "vcd cse" commands.
The resolution in my instance was to edit the "~/.vcd-cli/profiles.yaml" to add the cse extension as per the vcd-cli docs here:
https://vmware.github.io/vcd-cli/extending.html

I initially had the following in my file:

```
active: default
profiles:
```

Which I changed to:

```
active: default
extensions:
- container_service_extension.client.cse
profiles:
```

Once this was done I no longer received the "Error: No such command "cse"" errors.

To summarize this thread for any future readers, we currently only have two methods of running scripts. 

Path to Virtualenv
==============
The first is to use the virtualenv path to execute installed scripts, as you would with a traditional virtualenv. This value is retrievable from the `pipenv --venv` command which will produce the absolute path of the virtualenv regardless of environment variables.

e.g.
`/path/of/.venv/bin/python yourscript.py`
`/path/of/.venv/bin/installed_executable_script.py`


Pipenv run
========
If you want to use `pipenv` to run the job directly, you'll need to make sure pipenv is on the PATH of the cron user, navigate to the project directory, and use `pipenv run`. While this isn't an ideal interface, it's what we currently have as an option from cron'd tasks.

e.g.
`cd /path/to/project && pipenv run python yourscript.py`

If you have suggestions on how you'd like to see them improved, please feel free to create a new issue detailing how you'd envision it working. Thanks!

``` python
def _clip_float_to_range(arr, amin=-1, amax=1]):
    """
    Clips array-like object with .clip() attribute to the specified range.
    """
    try:
        return arr.clip(amin, amax)
    except AttributeError:
        # Misidentified array or error during clipping; return original value.
        return arr
    


def enforce_dtype_range(variable, amin=-1, amax=1):
    """
    Attempts to clip any floating point arrays to [-1, 1].
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    variable : Python object
        Output from any function.
    amin : float
        Minimum allowed value. Default: -1.
    amax : float
        Maximum allowed value. Default: 1.
    
    Returns
    -------
    variable : Python object
        Clipped array or arrays in function output.
    """
    # Shortcut trivial case
    if variable is None:
        return
    
    # Single numpy array output
    if isinstance(variable, np.ndarray):
        if variable.dtype.kind == 'f':
            return _clip_float_to_range(arr, amin=amin, amax=amax)

    # For a single tuple, suspect multiple return values.
    # Look one level inside.
    if isinstance(variable, tuple):
        # Attempt to clip return values individually
        return tuple(_clip_float_to_range(x, amin=amin, amax=amax) 
                     for x in variable)

    # Unforseen case, simply pass through.
    else:
        return variable
```

Hi @leeeeeeo . The use of both the transform and its inverse comes from the following identity: if you have a transform ``f``, a source ``src`` and destination ``dest`` images, so that
```
f(src) = dest
```
then 
```
dest[x, y] = src[f^{-1}(x), f^{-1}(y)]
```
that is, the value of a pixel of ``dest`` is the value of ``src`` at  inverse transform of pixel coordinates. This is more easily understood if you take the example of a zoom factor (say, you want to blow up your image by a factor of 2, then for pixel values of the new image you look for pixel values of the src image at x/2, y/2).

We build against the earliest version of NumPy required, which should in
theory give you wheels that are compatible with any later numpy of the same
major version.

When I wrote patsy this was one of the possible uses I had in mind, so the internals have the machinery you need. However, it's not currently exposed in a way that you can use from outside the library, so making that possible would need some thought and familiarity with the internals.

The basic flow for converting a formula to a design matrix is:

1. We parse the formula string, producing a parse tree (`patsy.parse_formula.parse_formula` – this is a private API)
2. We run an evaluator over the parse tree, producing a `ModelDesc` object (`patsy.desc.Evaluator` – this is also a private API). `ModelDesc` is public, and the public API that coordinates these two steps is `ModelDesc.from_factor`.
3. The `ModelDesc` is passed into the design matrix building machinery.

To support lme4 formulas, you'd need the parser to know about how to parse the new `|` operator, and then somehow during the evaluation stage you'd need to extract the information about the fixed and random effects. IIRC the fixed effects – everything that's not part of a `|` expression – are basically handled like any other formula (so in patsy you'd represent them as a `ModelDesc`), and then for each `|` you'd have one `ModelDesc` to represent the random effects, plus some kind of representation of the factors that they're conditioned on.

The parser itself is extensible: `patsy.parse_formula.parse_formula` even takes an `extra_operators=` argument. The evaluator is also extensible – see `Evaluator.add_op`. But, neither of these APIs have ever actually been used for anything – they were added as part of some vague plan to eventually support lme4-style formulas, that never got followed up on.

So... it's not trivial, but it is possible.

echo "backend: TkAgg" > ~/.matplotlib/matplotlibrc

@Crissov thanks for being patient with us and working through this. _We intended to add "proper" unicode in 5.0 but we couldn't find a clean solution_. The issue is a little deeper than just adding the Unicode code points and then re-generating the web fonts. You said "it changes absolutely nothing for people who are (and keep on) using the PUA codes". You are absolutely correct. However, we have to think about this from not just a technical perspective. This feature has to be supported and documented. I'll use this [screen grab](https://unicode-table.com/en/sets/hearts-symbols/) of the "heart" symbols to illustrate some of the issues that we have ran into that led us to abandon this effort with version 5. 1. There are multiple "solid" hearts, which one is canonical? Is the canonical version chosen by appearance? By glyph name? Do we map multiple code points? If I put the wrong unicode heart in my page and Font Awesome doesn't work is that a bug or my mistake? 1. For our outlined styles Font Awesome Regular and Font Awesome Light it does not seem appropriate for those to map to any of the "visually solid" Unicode glyphs. We'd need to map the Light heart to "White Heart Suit"? So this means that changing between styles using CSS (using font-weight) or in a desktop program is not possible. If you wanted to go from FA Solid "heart" to FA Regular "heart" the code point would need to change. 1. If we chose "Black Heart Suit" (which would make sense if we map to "White Heart Suit" for solid) as the canonical version because it visually matches the icon in Font Awesome there is an expectation that we would have the other symbols from the group as well (club, diamond, spade). If would also place a constraint on these icons (they need to look like the typical symbols on a deck of cards) that may not be desirable. 1. For the icons that do not have an associated Unicode code point, this creates a documentation and problem for anyone that has decided to use this particular technique to show icons. If they are relying on fallback fonts to display standard Unicode symbols and the icon is non-decorative we will not have a solution for this. > Some of them are colorful emojis, but it is the author’s job to decide whether they can live with 1. that fallback and 2. the fact that some platforms do not support custom fonts for emoji characters at all. This level of user intervention/responsibility is atypical for Font Awesome. We want to continue to make things as easy as possible. This doesn't strike me as within the same spirit of what Font Awesome is. > My more recent issue #10245 is about a restricted version of FA that has removed all PUA assignments as well as all glyphs without a standard code point. The use case is similar. Authors would know that not everything FA offers would be available on this font file. This would be a heavy burden for authors. And what recourse do they then take if they need an icon that is not included in the Unicode code points? > I feel like I have to emphasize something that has been pointed out repeatedly in #222 before: it changes absolutely nothing for people who are (and keep on) using the PUA codes. I disagree with this. It adds layers of [complexity and decision points](http://uxmyths.com/post/712569752/myth-more-choices-and-features-result-in-higher-satisfac). The fewer options that we have for using Font Awesome the easier and more enjoyable it will be to use. I want to emphasize that this topic is not something that we take lightly. We've put a fair amount of thought into it and our team was not able to solve it (I would say we've failed to solve it/understand it). So in that vein, we'll need to table this when we can come back to it and give it the proper attention. We underestimated it from the beginning. Project management note: * #222 * #10245 * #10357 Will be used as reference when we begin working on this again.

I like the separation of the tree structure from the variable attributes, in a flat list which does not pollutes the tree.

We could even separate explicitely variables of the `TaxBenefitSystem` from decomposition nodes (algebraic sums) in two flat lists. This would help verification tools #281 and allow to re-use the description. We could store the shortname in `Variable` classes but not colors, which can be specific to each decomposition. We could rename `desc` to `label` and `shortname` to `short_label` to homogenize with `Variable` classes.

My version (to be challenged):

```yaml
decomposition:
  salaire_brut:
   - salsuperbrut
   - remboursement_transport
   - cotisations_employeur
   - allegement_fillon
   - credit_impot_competitivite_emploi
   - tehr

algebraic_sums:
  salaire_brut:
    color: 0, 99, 143
    label: Salaires bruts
    short_label: Sal. bruts

tax_benefit_system_variables:
  salsuperbrut:
    color: 0,66,92
  remboursement_transport:
    color: 0, 66, 92
  cotisations_employeur:
    color: 249, 208, 211
  allegement_fillon:
    color: 0, 171, 208
  credit_impot_competitivite_emploi:
    color: 0, 171, 255
  tehr:
    color: 0, 0, 0
```

For example `remboursement_transport` could become:

```python
class remboursement_transport(Variable):
    column = FloatCol
    entity_class = Individus
    label = u"Remboursement partiel des frais de transport par l'employeur"
    short_label = u"Remb. transport"

    def function(self, simulation, period):
        remboursement_transport_base = simulation.calculate('remboursement_transport_base', period)
        # TODO: paramètres en dur dans le code
        return period, - .5 * remboursement_transport_base
```

Generally, CRF losses are implemented using the DyNet primitives by the
caller in the host level since DyNet's overhead is supposed to be low,
making this feasible (although Python is extremely slow, so using one of
the other host languages is a better bet for things like CRFs).
Furthermore, there are problems with providing a specialized "Node"
implementation of the CRF loss. First, there are many CRFs- linear chain as
well as more general graphical structures. Even if you want to target just
sequence labeling problems, what is the clique structure? Second, how do
you represent the “supervision”? With a single gold structure? Or do you
require the caller to provide posterior distribution targets? (This is a
bit like the difference between "pickneglogsoftmax" and computing the cross
entropy KL between a target distribution on outputs and the modeled
distribution- that way you can back prop into both.)

Because there are not really obvious default answers to these questions
(people who want "structured attention", as has been proposed a couple
times in the last year, would not want to be constrained just to have
target sequences), and because DyNet is designed to give you the primitives
you need to implement a CRF, my feeling is that it does not make sense for
us to include a primitive "CRF loss" node in the core library. However, I
do think in performance critical applications, being able to write custom
code for things like this would be useful, and, in particular, it would
nice to be able to add "third party nodes" that individual projects could
define, as well as possibly a library of reference implementations for
things like basic linear chain CRF losses with single sequence supervision.
The problem is: currently, nodes are defined in exactly one place-- inside
the core library.

Making "third party" nodes accessible from C++ host programs is easy enough
since C++ is always compiled and linked against many libraries, so you
could have the core dynet library, and then any additional libraries of
nodes (although there would be some problems with adding autobatching since
the core library maintains a list of all the batchable types). But, making
third party nodes visible in Python (where you would really want them
because it would let you address Python's slowness with fast C++ code),
would be non trivial. Right now, for every node type, we write a lot of
boilerplate in a single monolithic Cython file, and there is a bunch of
internal state that the Python library maintains that lives just in that
file. There probably is a design that would let you have more dynamic nodes
(even if you still had to cythonize your own code), but this is far from
where we are right now. But, if we want to get serious about letting people
implement CRF nodes and accessing them from Python, this is the direction
DyNet needs to take.

Extending the syntax to include 'and' and 'or' in the if clause would make it possible to define a set of regions in the blend design space to approximate a watershed line that is not orthogonal to all axes. Because of the underlying data structures, I don't think that full boolean logic can be supported: the FeatureVariations table supports only 'or's between sets of 'ands'. For the implementation, each 'and'ed rule would be added to the current ConditionSet, and each 'or' would trigger the start of a new ConditionSet.  I don't see a way to support an 'and' between two parenthetical clauses that contain an 'or'. However, the current support is enough to do what Petr is describing, which certainly is useful.

I am going ahead and revising the AFDKO private T1 hint format to  a plist-compatible format. I very much like the proposal above from nlsp on April 1st, and am pretty much adopting that. The first pass of this is now checked in under the AFDKO Open Source site, and full build will be coming to the Adobe download site in a week or two.  As before, I consider this a temporary format, and will switch over to whatever the UFO spec developers work out, when that happens.  The format is documented as _hintFormat2_  in FDK/Tools/SharedData/FDKScripts/ufoTools.py. It looks like:
			<key><com.adobe.type.autohint></key>
    		<dict>
				<key>id</key>
				<string> <fingerprint for glyph> </string>
				<key>hintSetList</key>
				<array>
					<dict>
						<key>pointTag</key>
						<string> <point name> </string>
						<key>stems</key>
						<array>
							<string>hstem <position value> <width value></string>*
							<string>vstem <position value> <width value></string>*
							<string>hstem3 <position value 0>...<position value 5></string>*
							<string>vstem3 <position value 0>...<position value 5></string>*
						</array>
					</dict>*
				</array>

				<key>flexList</key>*
				<array>
					<string><point name></string>+
				</array>
			</dict>
The multi-letter options with a single hyphen prefix are non-standard, and the current parsing code is messy. I think we can easily justify the breakage.

def process_data_files_aux (data_directory, filename_list, ..., output_metadata):
    # use a 'profiled' version of process_data_files
    config = dict(gen=pr.process_id)
    process_data_files_profiled = pr.profiled(**config)(process_data_files)
    pr.enable_profiling()
    results = process_data_files_profiled(data_directory, filename_list, case_sensitive, keywords, robj_fast, robj_slow, output_metadata)
    pr.print_stats(sort='cumulative')
    pr.disable_profiling()
    return results

The above function is called as follows:

    pool = mp.Pool(processes = num_threads)
    results = [pool.apply_async(process_data_files_aux, args = (data_directory, fsublist, ... , output_metadata),) for fsublist in flist]

I don't think this is right either. The fundamental problem right now is, given an interpreter and no platform, the pex resolve method chooses Platform.current() instead of the platform of the given interpreter (which there is no sane public way to calculate with the pex API as is today - a wart from the initual manylinux PR).

As an aside, pex understands 'current', so this PR could be replaced by just passing `platform=platform`.

Either way, this should fail in CI unless I was hallucinating when I upgraded us to 1.4.4, which required the truncated platform workaround.

I managed to resolve the issue but I will give some background and the solution.
My development IDE is Visual Studio. I have python 2.7.13 32bit installed on my C Drive. I have visual studio plugin for Python Language Integration. I am not sure of this but I think this plugin also installs the 64bit version of Python. Or I might have installed the 64bit version of python27 myself.
When I create a python project within visual studio, it detects both versions of python and organises them in these things called Environment. So I have IronPython installed then that would also be picked up as an environment. In visual studio I can switch the different Environments and run the same python source files.

I installed JunitXml using
pip install junit-xml in commandline

But when I switched to Python64 environment then import JunitXml returned an error the interpreter as an unknown package.

Here is the fix:
To have python 64bit in visual studio recognise junit-xml, you need to install it within visual studio. In devStudio, right click on the 64bit python environment and select Install python package.-> easy install and type in junit.xml and run as administrator

Please note in other packages I have pipped in commandline have installed the package for both 32bit and 64bit python.

Any how my issue has been resolved.

The only reason for me using 64bit python is that it crashes less in Visual Studio on simple functions like print, etc.

~~~console
editor opens
change each 'pick' to 'edit'
save the file and quit

$ git push -f
~~~

Regarding forgetting ``error_if_generator`` or whatever, no that's up to the *library* authors (e.g. Autobahn) -- users (of the library) wouldn't need to remember that. This lets library authors decide which Deferred-returning functions are "special" (e.g. user-facing) instead of just "mysteriously" affecting every Deferred produced by anything using ``txaio``.

As to the technical aspects, I think it's possible: we can detect a "bare" generator function with ``inspect.isgeneratorfunction``, which will return ``False`` after decoration with ``@inlineCallbacks``. This will return ``True`` for ``@asyncio.coroutine``, but we can detect an asyncio coroutine with ``asyncio.iscoroutinefunction``.

> Is this a limitation of Python? 

Lack of support for long-running requests is a limitation of WSGI, not necessarily a limitation of Python itself. :)

> Have you looked at Go for components related to keeping a long running open socket?

*Disclaimer: all thoughts laid out here are my own opinion*

That's why the logger's built in Go! Go's great for low-level systems programming such as a log server, but it **sucks** for handling API requests, authn/authz integrations, serialization/de-serialization of web requests, crafting responses, database interactions, etc. Django excels at all those points. It's a kickass web framework for crafting APIs. There's just no equivalent of Django in Go that meets our needs, and rightfully so, because Go was never meant to solve that problem domain. :)

There are evented solutions like python-twisted, gunicorn or raw gevent which can address this specific issue in the controller, but the more *maintainable* and *extendable* solution would be to roll out logging to a component which is built to host and serve logs, and just let the controller hand off the work to that component. This is the micro-services architecture in play. This way we can knock off more features by recycling the same endpoints while keeping the scope of the controller focused and the line count low. For example, [`deis logs -f`](465) has been a long-running feature request which can take advantage of a sessions API from the logger!

The thought of pivoting 2 years of code over from Python to Go scares me. I can only expect it to be fraught with compatibility issues and limitations which we've come to rely on. :fearful:

#### Complication 2

Assuming that we keep the `Chain` and networking processes together as they are now, we need a solution for the following two things.

1. Avoid calls to `Chain` methods which would block and subsequently time out the networking processes.  Without separating them, I don't know how we can accomplish this beyond self enforced discipline which is error prone.
2. Expose access to the `Chain` to the JSON-RPC process.  Current known approach using `multiprocessing` Manager pattern doesn't appear viable because it will allow heavy request load from the JSON-RPC server to overload the networking process, causing timeouts.

The officially recommended way to install Curator will remain `pip install elasticsearch-curator` for the time being.

However, I can use [nuitka](http://nuitka.net) to compile Curator and its dependencies into bytecode, and then share those as .tar.gz and .zip (for windows).  The problem with this approach is that it is _extremely_ system dependent, as it bundles the libc and other libraries from the host system.  I have built Docker images which will create Curator binaries for Ubuntu 12.04 & 14.04, CentOS 6 & 7 (latest on each).  The resulting binaries will work on the system they came from, but are not necessarily guaranteed to work on minor versions in the RHEL/CentOS camp.  It would be trivial to make another image for those point versions, however, now that I have good Dockerfiles and scripts.  

We can make these binaries available for download through our regular channels.  While it _might_ be possible to make RPM/DEB packages of these tarballs, because the binary is _so_ system specific, I don't think that's a wise approach.  Too many possibilities of library conflicts.  It would lead to major headaches in attempting to guarantee compatibility which are easily rectified by building a new binary from a compatible Docker image.

When you run Elasticsearch 6.x it will not assume Json and throw error 406 instead. 

 (406, u'Content-Type header [] is not supported', {u'status': 406, u'error': u'Content-Type header [] is not supported'})

https://www.elastic.co/blog/strict-content-type-checking-for-elasticsearch-rest-requests

"Starting from Elasticsearch 6.0, all REST requests that include a body must also provide the correct content-type for that body.

In earlier releases of Elasticsearch, the content-type was optional, and if it was missing or not recognised, then the server would sniff the content and make an educated guess regarding the content-type. That will no longer happen in Elasticsearch 6.0 - every incoming request needs to have the correct content-type for the body it contains.

This ability to enforce strict content-type checking has existed since Elasticsearch 5.3 via the http.content_type.required configuration setting. In 5.x it is optional, and defaults to false, in Elasticsearch 6.0, that setting defaults to true, and there is no way to disable it."

I understand, but it can also go the other way around - exposing too much information in the `__str__` of the exception might lead to unwanted noise - some of the exception bodies can be quite huge. That's where we settled for a compromise to get one root reason and expose the rest via `.info`. Ideally that it what you should include in your logs when encountering elasticsearch exceptions.

   - The env var must be set before the context is created.
   - Some runtime environments unset all env vars.

@Carreau thanks for clarifying the intended usage of the different parts of this PR. 

I completely understand the general need for a REST API for nbconvert. In particular, the main usage case that I see is a hosted version of nbconvert that is run separately from the rest of the notebook server.

In general, I tend to think that the REST services of the notebook server are one of the project's "open standards" and we should evolve it conservatively and with very clear versioning semantics and documentation. At the same time, I want nbconvert to be able to move quickly and innovate.

Because of this, I would probably feel more comfortable with the server side parts of this being in nbconvert and used in the notebook server as a server extension. That would also make it very easy to develop a separate server that just does nbconvert REST calls. We have taken the exact same approach with the Git server extension that exposes a REST API to git.

For the classic notebook I am fine with the UI additions in this PR, if the server side stuff is moved to a server extension, the UI could remain and add a small amount of logic to see if the server side stuff is installed and enabled. It could also be added as a nbextension, but that is a pain. The only potential issue there is version mismatch between the UI and the server extension.

For JupyterLab usage, the UI would probably be developed as a JupyterLab extension (an npm extension) so any of these options are fine on that front.

I agree these things are mostly separate from the issues I was bringing up about the usability of nbconvert. However, my points about the usability still stand and I don't think that these proposed changes remove the need for us to consider those matters on their own.

The following is the create_fixed_address_from_cidr() method I mentioned earlier:
```
def create_fixed_address_from_cidr(self, netview, mac, cidr, extattrs):
        ip = obj.IPAllocation.next_available_ip_from_cidr(netview, cidr)
        return obj.FixedAddress.create(self.connector,
                                       network_view=netview,
                                       ip=ip,
                                       mac=mac,
                                       extattrs=extattrs,
                                       check_if_exists=False)
```
As you can see, it first calls next_available_from_cidr() which returns an object that represents a query for the next available IP. That query need to be sent to Infoblox via the FixedAddress.create() that follows (passed as the parameter, ip), which tells Infoblox to allocate the next available IP. 

The object that is returned by the FixedAddress.create() would then contain the allocated IP. When printed, you will see the humanly readable IP that you expect, e.g. 10.10.1.10 - i.e. the address that has actually been allocated.

Hope this helps.

For a bit of extra context, the problem involved 128-bit decimals, which work differently than 64-bit or less decimals, in version 1.0 use Python's arbitrary precision integers to unpack the larger decimal without overflow.  The issue stemmed from the `PyNumber_FloorDivide` working differently than I had expected with negative numbers (e.g. `-0.08//100` would floor to `-1` rather than `0`).  In version 2.0 the 128-bit decimals were completely reworked to not rely on the Python integers and arithmetic so this will hopefully not be an issue there, however, a test was added so that this doesn't become a regression in the new version.

More and more I think that re-testing the queue when a state is removed is a mistake.

It was put in there to try to guard against the case of a handler taking some action that negated the state but then a subsequent handler taking action as if the state were still set and that causing an error.  However, there's just as much of an issue if two handlers have `@when_not` conditions on a state and one of them adds the state in question.

Additionally, it doesn't seem like the framework can both guard against that case and ensure that all handlers get their chance to run when their conditions are met, and the latter seems like the more important goal.

Finally, if that sort of co-dependence exists between handlers, it seems likely to be a design flaw and even so the handler could always manually check the state again with `is_state` if absolutely necessary.

You should use either assign() or subscribe(), not both.

subscribe() makes your consumer join the group and request subscription of the given topics (not partitions, they are ignored), after rebalancing your client will be assigned a subset of partitions for the subscribed topics (at this point the client automatically calls assign() on the assigned partition list).

assign() starts consuming the given partitions immediately.

I've looked into this and tracked it down to the [tz.tzlocal.__isdst](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/tz.py#L128) function, and as far as I can tell, there is no good solution. The time "November 2, 2014, 1:00 AM Pacific" is an ambiguous representation and apparently defaults to the PST side.

I think that the best thing to do, moving forward, is to get a good sense of where the ambiguity can be alleviated and either document the right way to deal with it or provide additional methods/functions that can help with that. For example, if you just want to know the *current* local time zone, `utcnow()` should be able to pretty unambiguously tell you what the current time it is, and you can localize based on that. The problem is that `__isdst` operates on any datetime, and datetime objects without time zones are inherently ambiguous around DST transitions.

After chatting with Lonami about this on Telegram, we believe we've identified the issue (in short, there are multiple threads trying to read at the same time).  There is an immediate workaround available, and Lonami should be fixing the root cause bug for the next release.

Workaround:

1) Instead of initializing with `update_workers=1, spawn_read_thread=False` and using `client.idle()`,

2) Initialize with `update_workers=0, spawn_read_thread=True`, and then use `client.updates.poll()`

In other words, the "Using the main thread instead the ReadThread" example from http://telethon.readthedocs.io/en/latest/extra/basic/working-with-updates.html doesn't currently work with 0.16, and you can use the "Spawning no worker at all" approach in the meantime.

In order to overcome this I am passing volume paths to a function `docker_windows_path_adjust` as you can see paths like `/E/var/lib in the error`. This works fine. But if the command to be executed inside docker also contain windows path(`as in this error message we have python E:\........`) then we get error as as it is a linux based container. We can solve this problem by applying the same function to path passed in command to docker. It appears these macros are only defined if the following condition is met:

```
/* The ISO C99 standard specifies that these macros must only be
   defined if explicitly requested.  */
#if !defined __cplusplus || defined __STDC_FORMAT_MACROS
```

The above is directly from `/usr/include/inttypes.h` preceding all these macro definitions.

So, I worked around it by explicitly defining:

```
#define __STDC_FORMAT_MACROS
```

right before the include for `inttypes.h` in that file:

```
diff --git i/src/muz/base/dl_util.cpp w/src/muz/base/dl_util.cpp
index 88b4c398d..5dfbe66cd 100644
--- i/src/muz/base/dl_util.cpp
+++ w/src/muz/base/dl_util.cpp
@@ -31,6 +31,7 @@ Revision History:
 #include "muz/base/dl_rule.h"
 #include "muz/base/dl_util.h"
 #include "util/stopwatch.h"
+#define __STDC_FORMAT_MACROS
 #include <inttypes.h>
```

And now it compiles just fine.

I'm not sure why this is a problem all of a sudden. I'm OK with the workaround; though not sure if anything needs to be done on the Z3 side so others don't have the same problem.

@jkleiser To get any useful model out of fastText (and other word vector implementation) you have to use **much** more data. The more, the better. Gigabytes of it ideally. When you train the model you set quite a few parameters (even though you might not be aware of it when you use the example scripts). For example ``-minCount`` (which is set to 5 by default) says: include only words in the model that occur at least 5 times in the training corpus, otherwise we just drop them. So anything that has an occurrence less than 5 will not show up in the ``.vec`` file.

``-minn`` and ``-maxn`` determine the minimum and maximum length of char n-grams in which your input data is chopped up. The vectors for the chopped up words (think of them as fixed size "syllables") is what goes into the ``.bin`` file. Finally, when you look up a word that is *outside* the vocabulary you have trained on, a vector is generated on the fly by *simply computing the average* of the word vectors corresponding to the n-grams it is made up from (see [fasttext.cc, line 28-37](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/master/src/fasttext.cc#L28-L37)).

The reason for the two time_point entries is that a time_point measured on the system clock represents a date/time (measures wallclock time).
The other time_points measured on other clocks, (steady_clock, high_resolution_clock, other custom clocks) are measured from some arbitrary start time (e.g. when computer was turned on) and don't really work as a date.
Based on this I decided the best fit for matching was times on the system_clock going to a datetime, times on other clocks just being a time (as they measure some absolute time), and differences in time (duration) being measured as time deltas.
If you have any ideas of better ways to map the features I'd welcome suggestions as I can see where the confusion is coming from.

Beginning by trying to answer a few of the questions here!

1. What is the purpose of `get_all_certs_keys()`? That's a great question, because the folks who added it to the interface are not presently active on the project :).  It isn't called by Certbot at the moment. So I'm trying to think of what it might be needed for in the future:
  * For automatically managing security enhancements (a la #1123 ) , ask the installer which certs it's currently using so that we know which ones might need security setting tweaks (but perhaps the `RenewableCert` / lineage objects can do that for you anyway? The advantage of `get_all_certs_keys()` is that it would reflect any changes made to config files after Certbot last worked with them.)
  * For asking installers about certs that came from somewhere other than Certbot/Let's Encrypt, in cases where there's something we should do with or to them, like auditing for weaknesses in the future?
2. What should we do with it? It seems that either accepting a `list` or an `iterator` is least likely to cause breakage. Or if we don't find any compelling use cases (the ones above seem very speculative) perhaps we should just remove it from the interface.

We just released web3 [beta 13](http://web3py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/releases.html#v4-0-0-beta-13), I highly recommend upgrading.

I took a brief look at these changes:
https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/compare/master...palango:web3-ification2#diff-f21cc0dc64f5cbf240a380453b9be7c2R72

One thing that jumps out at me is the contract invocations, like:

```py
self.web3_proxy.functions.registerEndpoint.transact(endpoint)
```

(Where `self.web3_proxy` is a contract at `discovery_address`)

---

The way the new function invocations in web3.py work is like this:

```py
contract.functions.contractMethod(contract_args_here).transact(transaction_metadata)
```

So to transfer 3 token atoms to someone at 2 a Gwei gas price, the line would be:
```py
my_token.functions.transfer("0x1111111111111111111111111111111111111111", 3).transact({'gasPrice': 2 * 10**9})
```

Even if your contract function doesn't take any arguments, it must be called, like so:
```py
self.web3_proxy.functions.registerEndpoint().transact(endpoint)
```
_(I didn't see the ABI, but I suspect that `endpoint` is supposed to be an argument to `registerEndpoint`)_

---

I believe the issue is the following. When the job is killed, the monitor cleans up the task and object table entries in Redis for that job.

The local scheduler also attempts to update the states of the tasks in Redis. The relevant line is https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/aa07f1ce4e0cfc4ba7bd0b466d77522360910b68/src/local_scheduler/local_scheduler.cc#L625

Changing that `task_table_update` to `task_table_add_task` works around the issue. So does commenting out the line in the monitor that cleans up the task/object table. https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/aa07f1ce4e0cfc4ba7bd0b466d77522360910b68/python/ray/monitor.py#L432

Another approach is to allow the `task_table_update` command to fail. There's no reason that the local scheduler has to die when trying to update a task that has been removed.

There used to be a few words about this in the README, but I took them out b/c people were complaining they wanted to get directly to the meat and skip all the words :-) If you're curious: https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/5e661a21931009de6bb4e1247dca741f4bf2dcd0/README.rst

I'll hopefully have a chance to write up a definitive answer to this soon, but in the mean time the short version is that callback-based libraries like asyncio/twisted/tornado are [inherently difficult to use correctly](https://vorpus.org/blog/some-thoughts-on-asynchronous-api-design-in-a-post-asyncawait-world/) when compared to async/await-based libraries like curio/trio. Curio and trio are much more similar to each other; basically the difference between them is that curio exists because Dave dislikes asyncio and wants a sandbox to explore alternatives, while trio exists because I dislike asyncio and want to have something I can use to build and ship stuff. (Until I split off to write trio, I think I was the 2nd most active curio contributor after Dave? and that blog post I wrote was a major factor in curio's popularization; trio is very strongly influenced by both the awesome ideas in curio and also the places where I felt it had weaknesses. The two projects remain on good terms :-).)

Guido's actually said that he'd like to see a curio/trio-style library added to the stdlib in 3.7 or 3.8... I'm not entirely sure I agree (for the usual reasons why adding stuff to the stdlib is a complicated trade-off – ["the standard library is where modules go do die"](http://www.leancrew.com/all-this/2012/04/where-modules-go-to-die/) etc.), but if we *are* going to do that then trio is basically my proposal for what it should look like.

We could call them "portals" maybe, retreating from the grim science fictional present into a pastoral fantasy past. Or, you know, Aperture Science 🍰.

When we get to implementing these interop mechanisms with other event loops (e.g. this already came up with kivy in #267) then there are two natural approaches. We could have one object that acts as a portal from trio→asyncio and a second object that acts as a portal from asyncio→trio, so something like `in_trio.run(...)` and `in_asyncio.run(...)`. Or we could have a single object that acts as a bidirectional portal, `portal.run_in_trio(...)`, `portal.run_in_asyncio(...)`.

The subtlety here is that internally, a bidirectional portal probably wants to capture a trio run and an asyncio loop, and use them in both directions. OTOH a unidirectional portal can use the ambient loop on the sending side, and only has to capture some representation for the receiving side. In principle this makes the unidirectional version a little more elegant, and it's more generic in some sense. But I'm dubious that this is enough to make up for the extra conceptual cost of having two objects to initialize and keep track of.

The only way to capture a trio loop is by running something in trio. This could end up being a bit awkward if any other libraries have the same constraint, b/c then how do you create a bidirectional loop? OTOH I don't think any other libraries have the same constraint. (And it wouldn't be hard to work around in trio either; really it's just that it would want to call `trio.hazmat.current_call_soon_thread_and_signal_safe` for you.)

I guess a kind of elegant design would be to have a generic `Portal` and then you plug together two "endpoint" classes (nothing to do with twisted endpoints, this would basically just be some kind of generic abstraction over different library's ideas of `call_soon`). The challenge here would be if one library has a different idiomatic API though, like maybe the twisted `run_in_trio` method should return a deferred instead of being an async method? (GUI libraries in particular mostly don't have async/await integration at all.)

We don't actually have to decide any of this now. I'm just trying to think it through a bit because maybe it effects what we want to call the methods for entering trio from a generic blocking thread. This is inherently unidirectional because you can't assign work to a random generic thread that doesn't have an event loop running in it, but maybe for consistency the names should still be `run_in_trio`, `run_sync_in_trio`? Though, eh, I guess consistency here is not really that important anyway. Most people are going to be using a blocking "portal" *or* an asyncio "portal", not both, and not together.
With this functionality merged, I think this issue should be closed and we should open a doc issue on documenting anonymized usage tracking and being upfront about it.
